title: API Server with Gin
description: Build a REST API using the popular Gin framework. Handle GET and POST requests gracefully.

steps.title: Setup the Project 🚀
steps.content:
To build an API, we'll use a popular third-party framework called **Gin**.

First, initialize a new Go module in an empty directory:
`go mod init my-api`

Next, install the Gin package:
`go get -u github.com/gin-gonic/gin`

steps.title: The Data Model 📚
steps.content:
Let's build a simple inventory API for a bookstore.

Create a `main.go` file. Define a `Book` struct with JSON tags.

```go
type Book struct {
    ID     string  `json:"id"`
    Title  string  `json:"title"`
    Author string  `json:"author"`
    Price  float64 `json:"price"`
}
```

The `` `json:"id"` `` tags tell Go how to serialize this struct into a JSON string when we send it to a client.

steps.title: Mock Database 🗄️
steps.content:
Create a slice of `Book` structs to act as our in-memory "database" for this exercise.

```go
var books = []Book{
    {ID: "1", Title: "Go in Action", Author: "William Kennedy", Price: 34.99},
    {ID: "2", Title: "The Go Programming Language", Author: "Alan Donovan", Price: 39.99},
}
```

steps.title: Handling a GET Request 🌐
steps.content:
Create a function named `getBooks(c *gin.Context)` to handle the `/books` route.

Gin makes sending JSON incredibly easy. Use `c.IndentedJSON(http.StatusOK, books)` to respond with a 200 OK status code and the `books` slice formatted nicely as JSON.

steps.title: Handling a POST Request 📩
steps.content:
Create a function named `addBook(c *gin.Context)` to handle new books.

1. Declare a new `Book` variable: `var newBook Book`.
2. Bind the incoming JSON request body to `newBook`: 
   `if err := c.BindJSON(&newBook); err != nil { return }`
3. Append the new book to the `books` slice.
4. Respond with `c.IndentedJSON(http.StatusCreated, newBook)`.

steps.title: Routing and Serving 🚦
steps.content:
Finally, inside your `main()` function, wire it all together!

1. Create a Gin router: `router := gin.Default()`.
2. Link the `/books` endpoint to the `getBooks` function: 
   `router.GET("/books", getBooks)`
3. Link a POST to the `/books` endpoint to the `addBook` function: 
   `router.POST("/books", addBook)`
4. Start the server on port 8080: `router.Run("localhost:8080")`.

steps.title: Testing your API 🛠️
steps.content:
Run your server using `go run main.go`.

Open a new terminal window to test it!

To GET books, use curl: 
`curl http://localhost:8080/books`

To POST a new book, send a payload:
`curl http://localhost:8080/books --include \`
`--header "Content-Type: application/json" \`
`--request "POST" \`
`--data '{"id": "3","title": "Learning Go","author": "Jon Bodner","price": 45.99}'`
